summary: Gruber endorses Steven Frank's critique of .dmg disk image files as a software distribution format on Mac OS X. Drawing on his own experience answering tech support emails for a Mac developer, he confirms that disk images are conceptually confusing for many non-technical users, even long-time Mac owners. He acknowledges that the trend of including background images with drag-to-install instructions helps, but agrees that compressed archives like StuffIt are more intuitive for most users.
key_points: Disk images (.dmg files) are conceptually confusing for non-technical Mac users, even those with years of experience
The trend of including instructional background pictures in disk image windows helps reduce confusion
Compressed archives like StuffIt are easier for most users to understand than disk images
The problem is especially acute for users who don't install new software frequently
money_quotes: The entire concept of a disk image is mighty confusing to many non-technical users, including many who have been using Macs for years, but don't install new software very often.

I think the recent trend of including a background picture in the disk image window, which instructs the user to copy the application icon to /Applications (or wherever), goes a long way to removing the confusion.
key_insight: Even longtime Mac users find disk images conceptually confusing, making software installation an unnecessarily frustrating experience that better distribution formats could solve.
